activities_projects.title: Tawarikh Author's Perspective Analysis
activities_projects.objective: Understand the bias and background of medieval chroniclers
steps: Identify the social background of the authors of Tawarikh (learned men, secretaries, administrators)
Analyze why they primarily lived in cities like Delhi and rarely in villages
Explain why they wrote histories for Sultans in anticipation of rich rewards
Discuss the 'ideal social order' they advocated, based on birthright and gender distinctions
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Critically evaluates the limitations and perspectives of medieval literary sources
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity in identifying the socio-political bias of Tawarikh authors (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Raziyya Sultan Debate
activities_projects.objective: Analyze gender politics in the 13th-century Delhi Sultanate
steps: Read Minhaj-i Siraj’s view on Raziyya (that a queen's rule went against the 'ideal social order')
Compare Raziyya's approach (being open about her gender) with Rudramadevi of the Kakatiya dynasty (who changed her name to act like a man)
Explain why the 'nobles' were not happy with her despite her being more capable than her brothers
Write a 100-word response on: 'Was Raziyya's failure personal or societal?'
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the historical challenges of women in leadership and contemporary social prejudices
activities_projects.evaluation: Depth of argument regarding gender and political authority (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Expansion Strategy: Internal vs. External Frontiers
steps: Identify the 'Internal Frontier' (clearing forests in the Ganga-Yamuna doab) and its economic purpose
Identify the 'External Frontier' (military expeditions into South India) starting with Alauddin Khalji
On a map, trace the route of Alauddin Khalji’s campaign to the South
Explain the significance of 'Garrison Towns' in maintaining control over these frontiers
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Distinguishes between consolidating existing territories and expanding into new regions
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of map tracing and explanation of the two frontier types (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Circle of Justice: Logic and Ethics
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the interdependence of the state and the peasantry
steps: Deconstruct the quote: 'A king cannot survive without soldiers... soldiers without salaries... salaries without revenue...'
Explain the ethical requirement: Why does the circle only work if the king promotes 'justice and honest governance'?
Analyze what happens to the circle if the peasantry is oppressed
Discuss if this 'Circle of Justice' is still relevant for modern governments
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the socio-economic theory of governance in the medieval period
activities_projects.evaluation: Logical flow of the circle and interpretation of 'Justice' (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Iqta System Management Audit
activities_projects.objective: Understand the administrative checks on the Muqtis
steps: Define the roles of the 'Muqti' (military commander and governor of an Iqta)
List three ways the Sultans kept the Muqtis under control (transfers, accountants, fixed soldier counts)
Identify why accountants were appointed by the state to check the Muqtis
Explain how a Muqti could become a threat to the Sultan if these checks failed
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes the methods used by central authorities to prevent provincial rebellion
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct identification of administrative checks and balances (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Comparative Crisis Management: Khalji vs. Tughluq
activities_projects.objective: Evaluate the responses to the Mongol threat
steps: Compare the defense of Delhi: Alauddin’s construction of 'Siri' vs. Muhammad Tughluq’s evacuation of Delhi
Analyze the 'Token Currency' experiment: Why did it fail compared to the traditional coins?
Evaluate the 'Price Control' system of Alauddin Khalji and its impact on the army
Conclude who was a more successful 'manager' of the empire during wartime
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Critically compares the administrative and military strategies of two major Sultans
activities_projects.evaluation: Evidence-based comparison of administrative successes and failures (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Mosque Architecture and Social Cohesion
activities_projects.objective: Identify the political and religious role of congregational mosques
materials: Images of Quwwat al-Islam and Jama Masjid
steps: Explain why the Sultans built many mosques in cities (to claim to be protectors of Islam)
Define 'Khutba' and its importance during the Friday prayers
Describe the architectural shift: How the Sultans used local materials and craftsmen to build these structures
Draw the plan of a mosque, labeling the Qibla and the Mihrab
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes how architecture was used to foster a sense of community and political legitimacy
activities_projects.evaluation: Technical labeling and understanding of the mosque's social function (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Impact of Mongol Invasions
activities_projects.objective: Trace the influence of external threats on Delhi's administration
steps: Note the frequency of Mongol attacks during the reigns of Alauddin and Muhammad Tughluq
Explain how the need for a 'large standing army' forced changes in the tax system
Identify the 'Ganga-Yamuna Doab' tax increase and its consequences (famine/rebellion)
Analyze how the 'Mongol Threat' shaped the boundaries of the Sultanate
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands how foreign policy and defense needs dictate domestic administration
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity in linking external threat to internal tax policies (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: From Chieftain to Subordinate: The Change in Power
activities_projects.objective: Examine the loss of autonomy for local rulers
steps: Research how Alauddin Khalji forced local chieftains to pay taxes instead of collecting them
Explain the 'measurement of land' system introduced for revenue collection
Describe the three types of taxes: on cultivation (kharaj), cattle, and houses
Analyze how this centralization weakened the local 'Samanta' or 'Aristocracy'
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the transition from decentralized local rule to a centralized bureaucratic state
activities_projects.evaluation: Accurate description of tax types and the shifting power of chieftains (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Sayyid and Lodi Transition
activities_projects.objective: Understand the fragmentation and eventual collapse of the Sultanate
steps: Identify the rise of independent kingdoms (Jaunpur, Bengal, Gujarat) after the Tughluqs
Explain why the Lodis were known as 'Afghan' rulers
Research Sher Shah Suri’s brief rule and how he influenced the later Mughal administration
Analyze the factors that made the Sultanate vulnerable to Babur’s invasion in 1526
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the period of decline and the emergence of regional powers
activities_projects.evaluation: Understanding of the political fragmentation before the Mughal era (10 marks)
